description:
You are a data-engineering assistant.  
    Inside the home directory there is a miniature ETL workspace with one raw data file that was exported from an on-line shop.  
    Your goal is to carry out the “T” (transform) step of a very small pipeline by using basic Linux command-line utilities (e.g. grep / awk / cut / sed / mkdir / tee / etc.).  
    The entire job can be finished with only three or four commands.

    1. Source data  
       • File path: /home/user/etl/input/sales_data.csv  
       • Format: plain UTF-8 CSV, comma separated, **no quotes**, first line is the header.  
         Columns: order_id,customer_id,date,product,quantity,unit_price  

    2. Required transformation  
       • Keep only the rows whose date field begins with the string “2021-”.  
       • Add one new column, revenue, as the last column.  
         revenue = quantity * unit_price  
         • unit_price is already in decimal form with two digits after the point.  
         • revenue must also be written with exactly two digits after the point (use normal rounding rules if needed).  
       • Preserve the original column order and header, simply append “,revenue” to the header line and the calculated value to every data line kept.

    3. Output artefacts that must be created  
       a) /home/user/etl/output/sales_2021_enriched.csv  
          • Must contain exactly four lines: one header line plus the three 2021 records in the original order.  
          • Exact byte-for-byte expected content (including trailing new-lines) is:

            order_id,customer_id,date,product,quantity,unit_price,revenue
            1002,502,2021-01-05,Mouse,5,25.50,127.50
            1003,503,2021-03-16,Keyboard,3,45.00,135.00
            1005,505,2021-12-30,Laptop,1,749.99,749.99

       b) /home/user/etl/output/summary.txt  
          • A single line, no extra spaces or EOL characters after it:  
            rows_processed=3

    4. Operational requirements  
       • Create the directory /home/user/etl/output if it is missing.  
       • Do not modify the original input file.  
       • Solve everything with ordinary shell tools; do NOT write or invoke a separate Python, Perl, etc. script.  
       • The entire solution should reasonably fit within 3–4 simple shell commands.

    5. What will be checked by the automated grader  
       • The presence of /home/user/etl/output/sales_2021_enriched.csv and /home/user/etl/output/summary.txt.  
       • Both files must exactly match the byte sequences shown above (including new-lines and lack thereof where specified).  
       • The original file /home/user/etl/input/sales_data.csv must remain unchanged.

    When you are ready, go ahead and run the necessary terminal commands to perform the transformation.
truth:
<!--
    Pre-existing directory structure
    /home/user/etl
        └── input
            └── sales_data.csv
    The grader creates those objects before the task starts.
    -->

    <initial_files>
        <file path="/home/user/etl/input/sales_data.csv"><![CDATA[
order_id,customer_id,date,product,quantity,unit_price
1001,501,2020-11-10,Laptop,2,799.99
1002,502,2021-01-05,Mouse,5,25.50
1003,503,2021-03-16,Keyboard,3,45.00
1004,504,2020-07-23,Monitor,1,189.99
1005,505,2021-12-30,Laptop,1,749.99
]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <!-- Expected artefacts after agent finishes -->
    <expected_files>
        <file path="/home/user/etl/output/sales_2021_enriched.csv"><![CDATA[
order_id,customer_id,date,product,quantity,unit_price,revenue
1002,502,2021-01-05,Mouse,5,25.50,127.50
1003,503,2021-03-16,Keyboard,3,45.00,135.00
1005,505,2021-12-30,Laptop,1,749.99,749.99
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/etl/output/summary.txt">rows_processed=3</file>
    </expected_files>
